specification.topic-statement: The viewport scroll action system adjusts the scrollback viewport offset in response to page, mouse scroll, and arrow input events, and drives completion and cursor navigation when the viewport is at the bottom.

scope.in-scope: Page-up and page-down actions, scroll-up and scroll-down actions (mouse wheel at 3 lines per event), arrow-up and arrow-down contextual routing, maximum offset calculation, and the scroll detector state machine that distinguishes mouse-wheel bursts from keyboard arrow presses.
scope.boundary: When the viewport is at the bottom and a completion is active, arrow keys delegate to the completion navigation system. Completion state management is handled externally.
When the viewport is at the bottom and no completion is active, arrow keys delegate to input buffer cursor movement. Cursor tracking is handled externally.

field.name: offset = 0
field.note: bottom of document visible (most recent content)

field.name: offset = N
field.note: scrolled N rows upward from the bottom

field.name: offset = max
field.note: top of document visible; max = document_height - terminal_height (or 0 if fits)

behavior.maximum-offset-calculation: Before any scroll action that increases the offset, the maximum allowable offset is calculated. Input buffer and scrollback layouts are synchronized to the current terminal width. The document height is computed as the sum of all layer heights. If the document fits within the terminal, the maximum offset is zero. Otherwise it is document_height minus terminal_height.
behavior.page-up: One full terminal height (in rows) is added to the current viewport offset. If the result exceeds the maximum offset, it is clamped to the maximum. The viewport now shows content one full screen height higher.
behavior.page-down: One full terminal height is subtracted from the current viewport offset. If the offset is already less than one terminal height, the offset is set to zero. The viewport moves one full screen height toward the bottom.
behavior.scroll-up: Three rows are added to the current viewport offset. If the result exceeds the maximum offset, it is clamped to the maximum. This corresponds to one mouse wheel scroll event upward.
behavior.scroll-down: Three rows are subtracted from the current viewport offset. If the offset is less than three, it is set to zero. This corresponds to one mouse wheel scroll event downward.
behavior.arrow-up:
The action is routed based on the current viewport state:
- If the viewport offset is greater than zero (the user has scrolled up from the bottom): scroll-up is performed (adds 3 rows to offset). - If the viewport is at the bottom and a completion is active: the completion moves to the previous candidate. - Otherwise: the input buffer cursor moves up one row.
behavior.arrow-down:
The action is routed based on the current viewport state:
- If the viewport offset is greater than zero: scroll-down is performed (subtracts 3 rows from offset, clamping to zero). - If the viewport is at the bottom and a completion is active: the completion moves to the next candidate. - Otherwise: the input buffer cursor moves down one row.
behavior.scroll-detection:
Arrow-up and arrow-down events are intercepted before dispatch and passed through a state machine that detects mouse-wheel scroll bursts. The state machine uses a 5-millisecond threshold between consecutive arrow events:
The first arrow event starts a timer. If a second arrow event in the same direction arrives within 5 milliseconds, the state machine enters the absorbing state and emits a scroll event. If 5 milliseconds elapse without a second event, the buffered arrow is released as a keyboard arrow action.
Additional arrow events within 5 milliseconds of the last absorbed event are silently consumed (the scroll was already emitted). The state machine returns to idle after 5 milliseconds without an event.
When any non-arrow, non-unknown event arrives, any buffered pending arrow from the waiting state is flushed and processed as a keyboard arrow action before the new event is handled.
When the select() call times out, the scroll detector's timeout check may release a buffered pending arrow and trigger the corresponding viewport action.
